demographic_anchor.name: Sandra Kowalski
demographic_anchor.life_stage: mid-to-late career professional, homeowner suburban, empty nester
demographic_anchor.education: bachelor's degree — business administration, Ohio State University
demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — HR director, regional healthcare organisation

life_stories.title: The newspaper at the kitchen table
life_stories.event: Sandra grew up in Akron, Ohio, in a household where the Plain Dealer arrived every morning and was read by both parents before work. Her father would read the front section and her mother the local news. On Sundays they'd spend an hour on the paper together over coffee. Sandra started reading it at 12 — first the comics, then the sports page, then the opinion section. She has associated reliable news with a physical ritual ever since. The digital transition was gradual and always felt like a compromise.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Deep habitual engagement with news — sees it as a morning anchor. Nostalgia for print and a residual distrust of the pace and format of digital news. Trusts outlets with long institutional records.

life_stories.title: HR director during the pandemic
life_stories.event: Managing HR for a healthcare organisation during COVID-19 was the most information-intensive period of Sandra's career. She was constantly monitoring CDC guidance, state health department updates, federal employment law changes, and local news about hospital capacity. She developed an intense ability to cross-reference official sources and became deeply impatient with speculation masquerading as fact. She watched colleagues share Facebook posts about policy changes before official guidance was released and get it badly wrong.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Strong preference for official, primary-source institutional news. Deep scepticism of social media as a news vehicle. Has strong trust in established institutions (CDC, AP, Reuters, local TV affiliates). Views social media news as professionally dangerous.

life_stories.title: The political shift in her social circle
life_stories.event: Over the past three years, Sandra has watched her social circle fragment along political lines. Two couples she and her husband had been close to for a decade stopped inviting them to things after a disagreement about a local election. She found the news increasingly difficult to discuss at work — HR director means staying neutral, which was fine professionally but personally isolating. She started reading news later in the day to avoid it affecting her morning at work.
life_stories.lasting_impact: News consumption increasingly private. Has shifted from discussing news with peers to consuming it solo. Avoidance is occasional and linked to political exhaustion rather than overload. Values outlets she perceives as non-partisan.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: rarely — habitual consumer; occasional avoidance during political peaks

primary_platform.label: local TV news + Columbus Dispatch digital + NPR radio morning

coping_mechanism.description: Sandra manages news-related uncertainty through ritual and institutional trust — reading the same sources at the same time, anchoring to organisations with long track records. Deviating from the routine feels uncomfortable.

key_tensions: Civic duty orientation demands engagement but political polarisation of news ecosystem makes engagement increasingly unpleasant
High institutional trust is increasingly strained as she perceives even 'quality' outlets drifting toward partisan framing

narrative: Growing up, the newspaper was as much a part of the kitchen table as the coffee pot. My dad read the front section, my mum read local news, and eventually I'd read the opinion pages whether I agreed with them or not. That habit never went away — I just went digital. I start every morning with the Columbus Dispatch app and NPR in the car. Local news first, then national. I trust news organisations that have been around long enough to have a track record of getting things wrong and correcting themselves. What I cannot stand — and I saw this in painful detail during COVID — is when people share Facebook posts claiming to be news. I managed HR for four hundred people during that period; I watched rumours about mandate policy spread faster than official guidance and create absolute chaos. That cured me of social media news permanently. YouTube for news? I don't really go there. That's for my grandchildren to show me things or for me to look up how to fix something around the house. My news engagement has become more private over the last few years. It used to be something you'd discuss — now political conversations can end relationships, and in my role I have to be careful. I still vote, still care, still read. But I'm more likely to sit with what I know quietly than to argue about it.
decision_bullets: Morning news routine is habitual and non-negotiable — local TV, radio, digital newspaper.
YouTube is not used for news — associated with entertainment and instructional content only.
Social media news use is extremely low — COVID-era misinformation experience created permanent aversion.
Trust anchors are institutional: local outlets, NPR, AP, Reuters — track record is the criterion.
Podcast news use is minimal — radio fills the audio news slot already.
Will pay for local news subscription — sees it as civic infrastructure, not a product.
News avoidance is rare and politically triggered — manages exhaustion by avoiding commentary, not news itself.
AI news summaries are resisted — human editorial accountability is a non-negotiable value.

core_memory.identity_statement: HR director, Columbus OH, $95k homeowner, habitual morning news consumer, strongly institutional trust, no social media news.

life_defining_events.event: Daily newspaper habit formed at family kitchen table in Akron
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: News as morning anchor ritual; print-origin consumer adapted to digital with residual format preferences

life_defining_events.event: HR director during COVID; watched social media misinformation create HR crises
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Permanent scepticism of social media as news source; strong institutional trust

life_defining_events.event: Social circle fragmented along political lines; news discussion became professionally and socially risky
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: News consumption increasingly private; preference for perceived non-partisan outlets

key_influencers: local TV anchors she's watched for years

immutable_constraints.budget_ceiling: willing to pay up to $20/month for quality news
